elements.sent-2411-001: In the present note we focus on dynamics on the Gehman dendrite $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is well-known that the set of its endpoints is homeomorphic to a standard Cantor ternary set.
elements.sent-2411-003: For any given surjective Cantor system $\mathcal{C}$ we provide constructions of (i) a mixing but not exact and (ii) an exact map on $\mathcal{G}$, such that in both cases the subsystem formed by $\text{End (\mathcal{G )$ is conjugate to the initially chosen system on $\mathcal{C}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The dynamics on the Gehman dendrite $\mathcal{G}$ is relatively well understood and leads to interesting examples.
elements.sent-2411-005: The authors of \cite{Kocan provided an example of a map $F\colon \mathcal{G}\to\mathcal{G}$ such that the subsystem $(\text{End (\mathcal{G ), F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G ) )$ is conjugate to a full shift on two symbols.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: By the well known characterization of Gehman dendrite \cite{Charatonik it immediately leads to examples of map $F$ with any infinite subshift without isolated points conjugate to $F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G )}$, e.g. see \cite{Li .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: These maps are not transitive, which leads to the question that motivates this paper: \begin{que Which Cantor dynamical systems can arise as invariant sets of transitive maps on the Gehman dendrite? \end{que Before providing an answer, let us recall a few related questions.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In \cite{Bald01 , Baldwin asked if the infimum of entropies of transitive maps on any dendrite which is not a tree is zero (it is known that it is positive on trees) and if zero is attainable by a transitive map on any dendrite.}
elements.sent-2411-009: He also developed a generalization of kneading theory, to describe dynamics on some dendrites \cite{Bald07 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The question of Baldwin was partially answered by \v{S pitalsk\'y in \cite{Spi , who proved that if $D$ is a non-degenerate dendrite such that no subtree of it contains all free arcs of $D$ then $D$ admits a transitive (or even exact) map with arbitrarily small entropy.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Clearly, the Gehman dendrite is a special case of dendrites covered by this result.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is also worth recalling another result by \v{S pitalsk\'y \cite{Spi2 , which shows that on Gehman dendrite transitive maps always have positive entropy.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The above considerations somewhat touch the surface of a more general question: what different types of chaos (and how many of them at the same time) can we observe in a dynamical system? There are many classical variations of chaos we may consider, e.g. Devaney chaos, Auslander-Yorke chaos, but also Li-Yorke chaos, $\omega$-chaos, distributional chaos and many more.
elements.sent-2411-014: By studying invariant subsystems in the dendrite case, we shed a light onto a subject of admittable chaotic behavior in the local sense.
elements.sent-2411-015: What's more, we take our considerations even further, setting the goal to look for global chaotic properties as well.
elements.sent-2411-016: The Cantor systems has been one of the key research interests in dynamics over the recent years.
elements.sent-2411-017: Notable advances in this topic include e.g. a study of generic maps in the space of homeomorphisms $\mathcal{H (\mathcal{C )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: It has been shown by Glasner and Weiss in \cite{Glasner that this space contains a dense conjugacy class.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This result has been presented also in \cite{monografia , in which the authors asked whether $\mathcal{H (\mathcal{C )$ contains a comeager conjugacy class.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: A positive answer to that question has been given by Kechris and Rosenthal in \cite{Kechris and later extended by many others, including \cite{Akin, Darji or \cite{Kupka to name a few.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These result show that in fact there is one typical map on Cantor set which is far from being chaotic of any type.
elements.sent-2411-022: Fortunately, its complement contains a plethora of admissible dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-023: From that point of view especially significant is the theory of symbolic extensions for dynamical systems, a topic that arose from a statement included in \cite{Weiss : every homeomorphism of a compact metric space admits a zero-dimensional extension with the same topological entropy.}
elements.sent-2411-024: This theory has been thoroughly studied by Downarowicz et al. over the last two decades, see e.g. \cite{Boyle,Downarowicz .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Let us now turn to answer to Question~\ref{q1 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The key ingredient is to properly define the action on the branching points of $\mathcal{G}$ and then extend the action onto edges of $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: The natural extension of $F$ onto the set $\text{End (\mathcal{G )$ will result with the subsystem $(\text{End (\mathcal{G ), F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G ) )$ conjugate to the Cantor system of choice.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-030: Then there exists a pure mixing dynamical system $(\mathcal{G , F)$ such that the subsystem $(\text{End (\mathcal{G ), F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G ) )$ is conjugate to $(\mathcal{C , f)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The construction provided in the proof of Theorem A can be modified to obtain an exact map on the Gehman dendrite while preserving the conjugacy of the endpoint subsystem.
elements.sent-2411-032: It is based on a result from \cite{Nadler (see Theorem~\ref{GMT ) which utility in construction of exact maps we learned from Karasová and Vejnar (see e.g. \cite{Klara for their application of this method).}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This modification forces to improve our observable chaos list: we no longer have just Devaney chaos, but the more complex exact Devaney chaos (see \cite{exact_devaney for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Then there exists an exact dynamical system $(\mathcal{G , F)$ such that the subsystem $(\text{End (\mathcal{G ), F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G ) )$ is conjugate to $(\mathcal{C , f)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This motivates the following question which we leave for further research.
elements.sent-2411-036: Does there exists a transitive dynamical system $(\mathcal{G , F)$ such that the subsystem $(\text{End (\mathcal{G ), F|_{\text{End (\mathcal{G ) )$ is conjugate to $(\mathcal{C , f)$ and topological entropy satisfies $h_\text{top (f)>h_\text{top (F)-\varepsilon$? \end{que In other words, can any Cantor dynamical system be extended to transitive dynamical system of the Gehman dendrite without increasing entropy more than by $\varepsilon$?.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For such TDS we define the following properties, characterizing its local behavior over time:.
elements.sent-2411-038: We say that a TDS $(X,f)$ is transitive if for each pair of nonempty open subsets $U,V\subset X$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $f^{-n (U)\cap V\neq\emptyset$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We say that a TDS $(X,f)$ is topologically mixing if for every nonempty open sets $U,V\subset X$ there exists $n_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n\geq n_0$ we have $f^{-n (U)\cap V\neq\emptyset$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We say that a TDS $(X,f)$ is exact if for each nonempty open set $U\subset X$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $f^{n (U)=X$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We often say that a map $f\colon X\to X$ is transitive (respectively mixing, exact) and by that we mean that the TDS $(X,f)$ has that property.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given the space $X$, there exist maps on $X$ which are mixing but not exact.
elements.sent-2411-043: We call those maps pure mixing.
elements.sent-2411-045: We say $(Y,g)$ is semiconjugate to $(X,f)$ if there exists a continuous surjection $\phi\colon X\to Y$ such that $\phi\circ f=g\circ\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Moreover, if $\phi$ is a homeomorphism, then we say that $(X,f)$ and $(Y,g)$ are conjugate.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section we will introduce the necessary notions to approximate Cantor dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-049: A map $\phi\colon V\to V'$ is a graph homomorphism if $(u,v)\in E\implies (\phi(u), \phi(v))\in E'$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Adopting the notation from \cite{Shimomura , whenever this is the case, we write $\phi\colon G\to G'$ or $\phi\colon (V,E)\to (V', E')$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $\phi\colon G\to G'$ be a graph homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-052: We say that $\phi$ is vertex-surjective if $V'=\phi(V)$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Similarily, we say that $\phi$ is edge-surjective if $E'=(\phi\times\phi)(E)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let $\phi\colon (V,E)\to (V', E')$ be a graph homomorphism.

elements.sent-2411-056: Then we say that $\phi$ is + directional.
elements.sent-2411-057: A graph homomorphism $\phi\colon G\to G'$ is called a cover if it is a + directional edge-surjective graph homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-058: Note that, following \cite{Shimomura , we will use the notion of a "graph cover" and "cover" interchangeably, assuming that these notions signify the same mathematical object.}
elements.sent-2411-059: We say that a family $\mathcal{U}$ of open subsets of the space $X$ is an open cover if $\bigcup_{U\in\mathcal{U U =X$.}}

elements.sent-2411-074: In what follows, we will explain how the structure of $\mathcal{C}$ can be used to define a Gehman dendrite with "marked" branching points and associate with this structure a mixing map $F$ with desired properties.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a Gehman dendrite, that is the topologically unique dendrite (up to homeomorphism) whose set of endpoints is homeomorphic to the Cantor set and whose branch points are all of Menger-Urysohn order 3 (see \cite{Charatonik, Nadler, Nikiel for further details).}
elements.sent-2411-076: We know that, with due diligence, $\mathcal{G}$ can be constructed as a closure of a union of countably many arcs in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Let us shortly present the main idea together with a suitable notation for our further considerations.
elements.sent-2411-078: We begin with an initial point $c\in\mathbb{R ^2$, which we will call the root of $\mathcal{G}$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: We attach two arcs $A_0 = [c, c_0]$, $A_1 = [c, c_1]$ in such a way that $(c, c_0], (c, c_1]$ are disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-080: Then we continue in the same way, attaching two arcs to each of the other endpoints of arcs attached in the previous step.
elements.sent-2411-082: Then for indexing sequence at step $n\geq 2$, $\omega=(\omega_0,\ldots,\omega_{n-1 )\in \{0,1\}^n$ and a corresponding arc $A_\omega=[c_{\omega{[0,n-2] ,c_{\omega ]$, where $\omega{[0,n-2] =(\omega_0,...,\omega_{n-2 )$ is the restriction of $\omega$ to the first $n-1$ digits, we attach two arcs $A_{\omega0 =[c_\omega,c_{\omega0 ]$ and $A_{\omega1 =[c_\omega,c_{\omega1 ]$.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-085: All arcs indexed with sequences of length $n$ will be called level $n$.
